Malaysia.AI Strategy: OECD AI Policy Observatory - Malaysia's Artificial Intelligence Roadmap 2021-2025 (launched in Aug 2022 by Malaysian Ministry of Science, Technology, and Innovation (MOSTI)) - - Malaysia's Artificial Intelligence Roadmap 2021-2025  describes how Malaysia’s AI capabilities will be harnessed, catalysed and propelled from 2021-25  - Vision: “Make Malaysia a nation where Artificial Intelligence augments JOBS, drives NATIONAL COMPETITIVENESS, encourages INNOVATION & ENTREPRENEURSHIP to bring economic prosperity, social good and improves PEOPLE’S WELL BEING”  - Mission: “To create a THRIVING NATIONAL AI ECOSYSTEM that allows EVERYONE (government, business, and people) to capitalise on the BENEFITS OF AI in a SECURED AND SAFE manner for economic prosperity and social well-being”  - Envisioned outcomes: 1. Establishing AI governance 2. Advancing AI R&D 3. Escalating digital infrastructure to enable AI 4. Fostering AI Talents 5.Acculturating AI 6. Kickstarting a national AI innovation ecosystem - 1. https://mastic.mosti.gov.my/publication/artificial-intelligence-roadmap-2021-2025/ - Information about AI strategy not available on OECD AI Policy Observator
Malaysia.AI Governance: UNESCO - Artificial Intelligence Readiness Assessment Report - The following guidelines are currently being used in the development and deployment of AI in Malaysia.  1. The National Guidelines on AI Governance and Ethics  2.ASEAN guide on AI Governance and Ethics - -The National Guidelines on AI Governance and Ethics are intended to establish Responsible AI practices as the government aspires to enhance the development and deployment of AI   - Guidelines are structured across three levels: Users, Regulators, and Developers  - Built on 7 AI Principles of fairness; reliability, safety and human control; privacy and security; inclusiveness; transparency, accountability, and human-centricity, the AIGE lays a strong foundation for future AI legislation - 1. https://mastic.mosti.gov.my/publication/the-national-guidelines-on-ai-governance-ethics/  2. https://asean.org/book/asean-guide-on-ai-governance-and-ethics/  3. https://www.unesco.org/ethics-ai/en/malaysia

Consumer Protection Act 1999 ensures marketplace safeguards 3. Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) 2010 - Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024 addresses Data protection and privacy  4. Computer Crimes Act 1997 tackles cybercrimes  PDPA Act's enforcement is overseen by the Department of Personal Data Protection (JPDP) - As per the UNDP Digital Development Compass, Malaysia has a score of 4.17 in data and privacy and  5 in cybersecurity. (5 is the top score representing transformational performance). It has dynamic Legal Framework: Comprehensive law, baselines, compliance, international alignment. - 1. https://digitaldevelopmentcompass.undp.org/country/MYS

Malaysia.Digital ID: DPI Map - Present - MyDigital ID - registration begin in 2024 / MyGOV Malaysia with a budget of RM80 million  Governed by Ministry of Digital - My Digital ID Sdn Bhd - MyDigital ID is a government initiative for a secure and trusted digital identity solution.   Identity verification refers directly to the government database, without storing any personal data and does not replace the MyKad system. It also facilitates access to online applications and portals using only one password. - 1. Malaysia.Digital Payments: DPI Map - Present - Real-time Retail Payments Platform (RPP) started in 2018 - • Real-time Retail Payments Platform (RPP) is a fast payment system launched in 2018) that enables Malaysian consumers, businesses and government agencies to make real-time, data-rich payments between accounts at participating financial institutions  • RPP was launched by PayNet, the operator of shared payments infrastructure formed by merger of MEPS (Malaysian Electronic Payment System) - an interbank network service provider in Malaysia and MyClear (Malaysian Electronic Clearing Corporation Sdn Bhd) - a wholly-owned subsidiary of Bank Negara Malaysia - 1. https://fastpayments.worldbank.org/sites/default/files/2021 09/World_Bank_FPS_Malaysia_RPP_Case_Study.pdf 2.DPI Map
Malaysia.Data Exchange: DPI Map - Present - Malaysian Government Central Data Exchange (MyGDX) - MyGDX is a seamless data sharing ecosystem that encompasses digital governance and a secured platform to empower and enhance end-to-end (E2E) online services towards a data-driven government. The implementation of MyGDX allows the sharing of classified data to be coordinated more efficiently and reduces the cost of infrastructure and system integration. A shared platform makes you quicker. - 1. Malaysian Government Central Data Exchange (MyGDX)

Cambodia.AI Strategy: OECD AI Policy Observatory - National AI Strategy 2025-30 is in the draft stage - 1. Vision -The vision of the strategy is to transform Cambodia into a skillful adopter of AI in the short term and a contributor to the global AI development market in the long term. -It aims to leverage AI as a primary driver for a digital economy and society, ensuring that technological advancement is human-centered, ethical, and rights-based.  2. Mission To achieve this vision, the mission focuses on building the foundational pillars of a trustworthy AI ecosystem through: -Developing Human Capital: Creating a workforce equipped with AI and data science skills. -Building Infrastructure: Establishing national data platforms, cloud-first policies, and AI labs. -Promoting Ethical Governance: Developing frameworks that ensure AI is used responsibly, inclusive of the Khmer language and cultural context. -Fostering Sectoral Adoption: Encouraging AI integration in key socio-economic sectors like education, healthcare, and agriculture.  3. Envisioned Outcomes The strategy outlines specific outcomes through its six strategic priorities: -Skilled Workforce: Increased number of AI professionals and researchers through specialized university programs. -Digital Government Excellence: Enhanced public service delivery through automated, streamlined, and transparent administrative processes. -Economic Competitiveness: Boosting SME efficiency and modernizing industries (e.g., agricultural resource optimization). -Khmer Language Inclusion: The successful development of a Khmer Large Language Model (LLM) to ensure AI services are accessible to all Cambodians. -HPC & Data Sovereignty: Establishing a National AI and Data Science Lab to reduce reliance on external compute resources and store data locally. -International Standing: Positioning Cambodia as a proactive participant in regional (ASEAN) and global AI governance discussions. - 1. Draft National AI Strategy - Jun 2025 2. Progress of AI Strategy Draft - Jun 2025
Cambodia.AI Governance: UNESCO - Artificial Intelligence Readiness Assessment Report - Cambodia scored 55 out of 100 in the Artificial Intelligence Readiness Assessment, with drafts of Legislation on Personal Data Protection and Cybersecurity yet to pass. - 1. Vision  To establish a human-centered, ethical, and rights-based governance ecosystem that positions Cambodia as a responsible adopter of AI in Southeast Asia. This vision aligns technological innovation with the Pentagonal Strategy – Phase I, ensuring that AI serves as a catalyst for sustainable development and social equity while safeguarding the fundamental rights and dignity of all Cambodian citizens.  2. Mission  The mission for AI Governance in Cambodia is to build a robust and inclusive regulatory environment through: -Legislative Grounding: Expediting the passage of foundational laws, specifically the Personal Data Protection Law and the Cybersecurity Law, to create a secure legal baseline for AI. -Ethical Standard Setting: Implementing a "soft law" approach initially—guided by the UNESCO Readiness Assessment Methodology (RAM) and the ASEAN Guide on AI Governance and Ethics—to promote transparency and accountability. -Multistakeholder Oversight: Fostering a coordinated institutional framework led by the Ministry of Post and Telecommunications (MPTC) that integrates government, private sector, and civil society into the governance process. -Khmer-Centric Inclusion: Ensuring that regulatory frameworks mandate the inclusion of the Khmer language and cultural context in AI datasets to prevent digital exclusion.  3. Envisioned Outcomes  By 2030, the implementation of these governance frameworks is expected to achieve: -Trust and Safety: A high level of public trust in AI systems, supported by clear mechanisms for liability, auditability, and redress in cases of AI-related harm. -Standardized Innovation: A "Sandbox" regulatory environment that allows startups to innovate while adhering to national safety standards, reducing the risk of biased or opaque algorithms. -Regional Alignment: Full harmonization with ASEAN AI governance standards, facilitating cross-border data flows and international investment confidence. -Institutional Clarity: An end to institutional fragmentation, with clearly defined roles for MPTC (Backbone & Governance), MISTI (R&D), and MoEYS (Education). -Data Sovereignty: The establishment of a National Open Data Platform and a National AI Lab that operate under strict ethical governance, ensuring data is used for the public good. - 1. UNESCO AI Readiness Assessment 2. AI Ethics report by UNESCO - Cambodia
Cambodia.Data Legislation: DLA Piper UNDP Digital Development Compass - Low amount of Data protection and Privacy regulation in Cambodia as reported by DLA Piper (Jan 2025). - the Royal Government of Cambodia issued Sub-Decree No. 252 on the Management, Use, and Protection of Personal Identification Data (only available in Khmer) (Sub-Decree 252) in order to promote broad policy objections, such as - ensuring the protection of peace and order; serving the public interest; and promoting national development by improving the provision of services. However, Sub-Decree 252 only applies to "personal identification data" owned by the Ministry of Interior (MOI) and does not apply to personal identification data used by other entities. In September 2023, the MPTC made available to select private organizations and companies a Draft Law on Personal Data Protection for their review and comment.  A later draft was made available to the Public for consultation in July 2025. - 1. DLA Piper - Cambodia 2. Public Consultation on Draft of Personal Data Protection Law
Cambodia.Government AI Initiatives: Government digital transformation portals, ministry annual reports, budget documents etc - As part of the draft of the National AI Strategy, Combodia has created the National Research Center on AI for Education, while for practical uses,  Landmine detection tools using AI are being built to achieve a Landmine free status by 2030.   Ministry of Post and Tele Communication is also developing a Khmer based LLM using the Llama-3 open source model. - NEC and the Cambodian Mine Action Center Successfully Predict Landmine: Traditionally, identifying potential landmine-infested areas required extensive, unorganized data and significant manpower over long periods. By leveraging AI, this process can now be performed quickly with fewer personnel, demonstrating the potential to support swift and efficient landmine clearance.   National Research Center on AI for Education inaugarated (Nov 2025): The National Research Center on AI for Education was officially inaugurated during the AI for Education National Conference 2025. The Inauguration Ceremony was presided over by His Excellency Dr. Hang Chuon Naron, Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Education, Youth and Sport (MoEYS), and His Excellency Dr. Chea Vandeth, Minister of Post and Telecommunications (MPTC). The establishment of the Center was led by the Rector Council of Cambodia (RCC), in cooperation with the Cambodia Academy of Digital Technology (CADT), and supported by MoEYS and MPTC.  Cambodia and Singapore Partner to Develop Khmer Large Language Model: AI Forum Cambodia and AI Singapore have signed an MoU to collaboratively develop an open-source Khmer Large Language Model, aiming to advance AI capabilities in Southeast Asia by addressing linguistic challenges, fostering skill development and ensuring equitable access to AI for Cambodians - 1. Landmine Detection using AI  2. National Research Center on AI for Education  3. Cambodia and Singapore - Khmer LLM
Cambodia.Digital ID: Cambodia Digital Economy and Society Policy Framework 2021-2035 and the Cambodia Digital Government Policy 2022-2035 - 1. Vision  -To build a vibrant digital economy and society by laying the foundations for promoting digital adoption and transformation across all sectors of society—the state, citizens, and businesses—to accelerate new economic growth and improve social welfare in the "new normal." -The long-term aspiration is to leverage digital technology as a catalyst to transform Cambodia into an upper-middle-income country by 2030 and a high-income country by 2050.  2. Mission The mission is to execute a 15-year roadmap (2021–2035) focused on three strategic pillars and two foundational layers: -Pillar 1: Digital Citizens: Fostering a pool of digital talent, enhancing digital literacy, and developing digital leadership among the Cambodian people. -Pillar 2: Digital Government: Transforming public administration into a "Smart Government" by modernizing public services and adopting data-based governance. -Pillar 3: Digital Business: Encouraging enterprises and SMEs to embrace digital technology and fostering a robust startup ecosystem. -Foundations: Building reliable Digital Infrastructure (high-speed internet, 5G, and data centers) and ensuring Digital Trustworthiness through robust cybersecurity and legal frameworks (Data Protection and Cyber Laws).  3. Envisioned Outcomes The strategy is implemented in three distinct phases, each with specific envisioned goals: -Phase 1 (2021–2025) – Building Foundations: * Establishment of the "National Council for Digital Economy and Society." -Successful deployment of digital identity (E-ID) and payment gateways (e.g., Bakong). -Enactment of essential digital laws and updated regulations.  -Phase 2 (2026–2030) – Digital Adoption: * Mass adoption of digital services by citizens and businesses. -Modernization of all key state systems and public services. -Strengthened regional (ASEAN) and global digital cooperation.  -Phase 3 (2031–2035) – Digital Transformation: * Transformation into a fully participatory digital society where technology drives economic productivity and innovation. -A sizable digital economy contributing significantly to the national GDP. -A highly skilled workforce specializing in advanced fields like AI, Data Analytics, and IoT.

Phillipines.AI Strategy: Government AI Readiness Index 2025 - Oxford Insights , OECD AI Policy Observatory - Adopted, National Artificial Intelligence Strategy Roadmap (NAISR) 2.0, 2024 (upversioned from NAISR 2021, by Department of Trade and Industry) - The National AI Strategy Roadmap (NAISR) 2.0, launched by the Philippine Department of Trade and Industry (DTI) on 3 July 2024, is an updated framework designed to position the Philippines as a regional "Center of Excellence" for Artificial Intelligence.  Key Objectives and Features Center for AI Research (CAIR): Launched alongside the roadmap, CAIR is the country’s first dedicated AI hub. It focuses on creating AI solutions for local challenges in sectors like sustainable agriculture, healthcare, and disaster resilience. Economic Impact: The strategy aims to boost the economy by up to PHP 2.6 trillion annually through AI integration and increased R&D spending (targeting a rise from 0.3% to 1.0% of GDP). Workforce Development: A core focus is the "whole-of-nation" approach to upskilling and reskilling Filipinos to ensure they remain competitive in an AI-driven global market.   The Seven Strategic Imperatives The roadmap is structured around seven key pillars to drive adoption and innovation:   - Environment: Building a robust, connected, and networked infrastructure. - Data: Improving data access and value extraction. - Education: Transforming the education system to nurture future AI talent. - Workforce: Large-scale upskilling and reskilling programs. - Ecosystem "Conscience": Developing ethical, safe, and responsible AI governance. - Research: Mastering and pushing the boundaries of AI through R&D. - Innovation: Accelerating the practical application of AI across industries. - 1. https://erikalegara.com/uploads/NAISR2.0_July2024.pdf - Information about AI strategy not available on OECD AI Policy Observator
Phillipines.AI Governance: UNESCO - Artificial Intelligence Readiness Assessment Report - Draft, Pending Appoval  Senate Bill no 25  Others Senate Bill no 852 House Bill No 7396 House Bill No. 807 House Bill no 0057 - Senate Bill No. 25, also known as the Artificial Intelligence Regulation Act (AIRA), was filed on July 2, 2025, during the 20th Congress of the Philippines. The bill proposes a comprehensive legal framework for the ethical, transparent, and accountable development and use of AI systems in the country.   As of July 29, 2025, the bill is pending in the Committee on Sustainable Development Goals, Innovation, and Futures Thinking after its first reading.   Key Provisions of SB 25 The bill aims to establish several new regulatory mechanisms and obligations:  National AI Commission (NAIC): A proposed central regulatory body with exclusive jurisdiction over AI. It would maintain a national AI registry, issue guidelines, and oversee enforcement. AI Ethics Review Board: A body within the NAIC responsible for issuing implementing guidelines and reviewing risks associated with AI applications.  - Risk-Based Classification: Mandates the registration and risk-based classification of AI systems, ranging from chatbots to deepfakes. - Labor Protections: Requires employers to provide advance notification and impact assessments if AI integration might affect labor rights or cause job displacement. - Prohibited Practices: Outlines illegal acts, including using AI to commit crimes, failing to disclose AI-generated content, and using AI to manipulate public opinion or exploit individuals. Penalties: Violators could face mandatory compliance training, license suspension, monetary fines (up to ₱10 million reported in some summaries), and imprisonment.   Context and Other Measures SB 25 is part of a broader legislative push to address AI risks. Other related pending measures include:  Senate Bill No. 852: The "AI Regulation Act" which proposes an "AI Bill of Rights". House Bill No. 7396: The "AI Development and Regulation Act of the Philippines," which seeks to create an AI Development Authority. House Bill No. 807: Known as the "Take It Down Act of 2025," focusing on criminalizing non-consensual AI-generated sexually explicit materials - 1. https://senate.gov.ph/legislative-documents/bills/594456
Phillipines.Data Legislation: DLA Piper UNDP Digital Development Compass - The Philippines’ data protection regime is governed by the Data Privacy Act of 2012 (Republic Act No. 10173), which regulates the collection, processing, storage, and transfer of personal data.  Core Framework: Data Privacy Act (DPA) - Applies to Personal Information Controllers (PICs) and Processors (PIPs), including certain extraterritorial processing involving Philippine citizens or residents. - Defines personal and sensitive personal information, with stricter safeguards for sensitive data. - Requires lawful bases for processing such as consent, contractual necessity, legal obligation, protection of vital interests, public authority mandate, or legitimate interests. - Imposes security, confidentiality, and proportionality obligations.  National Privacy Commission (NPC) The DPA established the National Privacy Commission as the independent regulator responsible for enforcement . The NPC oversees compliance, investigates complaints, conducts compliance checks, mandates DPO registration for covered entities, requires 72-hour breach notification where risk of harm exists, and has issued guidance on cross-border transfers and AI-related data processing.  Legislative Developments Amendment bills have been filed, including House Bill Nos. 892 and 898, House Bill No. 9651, and Senate Bill No. 1367, all seeking to revise aspects of the DPA. These remain pending, and there are currently no indications that they will be enacted within the next 12 months.

COARE High Performance Computing & AI Compute Support (National AI Research Infrastructure) – DOST-ASTI - AI-4RP (Project Gabay) AI-enhanced weather forecasting collaboration between DOST-ASTI, DOST-PAGASA and Atmo Inc. Produces higher-resolution forecasts to support disaster resilience and extended lead-time predictions.  SkAI-Pinas (Philippine Sky AI) and ASTI-ALaM (Automated Labeling Machine) National R&D program to build automated labeling and model pipelines for large local datasets, including ASTI-ALaM as an automated labeling component and a model store for reuse. Supports remote sensing, agriculture, and disaster applications.  iTANONG (NL interface for government databases) DOST-ASTI project to let users query relational databases in Filipino, English, or Taglish via a natural language interface, improving citizen access to government data. Originally started as a multi-year ASTI research project and referenced in DOST materials about the new national AI hub.  AI for Traffic / MMDA AI pilots (vehicle detection and intelligent signaling) Verified pilots and plans to use AI video analytics for vehicle detection and to modernize traffic signal control in Metro Manila. MMDA is adopting AI detection and planning intelligent signaling upgrades. Note: specific private products and pilots exist; I only list the MMDA / government confirmed activity here.  AINA (Artificial Intelligence Nutrition Assistant) Mobile AI system to identify foods from images and estimate portion sizes and nutrition metrics for use in public health research and practice. Documented by DOST PCHRD and partner publications.  UTAK AI (Clinical landing and federated learning for brain tumor imaging) Research project using AI for brain tumor detection and federated learning across clinical sites; listed in the Philippine health research registry and DOST/PCHRD summaries.  NAIRA / DIMER and AI-as-a-Service (AIaaS) prototype (DOST-ASTI) DOST-ASTI’s initiative to provide a shared AI platform and model exchange (DIMER) as part of the national AI infrastructure. ASTI has publicly described a prototype AI-as-a-Service roadmap and model store to let agencies and researchers deploy models.  National Artificial Intelligence Center for Research and Innovation (NAICRI / National AI Center) Announced DOST launch and hub plans to serve as the national research and compute backbone for AI R&D, training, and shared compute resources.  National Data Lakehouse and the GATES Program DOST’s GATES program to harmonize interagency datasets into an AI-ready data lakehouse for evidence based governance, research and cross-sector analytics. Public DOST/region and ASTI documents describe the Lakehouse building work.  Digital Bayanihan Chain (DICT blockchain for the 2026 GAA and budget transparency) DICT led initiative to put the national budget records onto a blockchain ledger to create tamper-proof, auditable budget traces. Confirmed in DICT and national press releases about the 2026 budget integration.  ₱1.5 Billion AI upskilling allocation and DepEd AGAP.AI / AI literacy programmes Government commitment and reporting of a ₱1.5 billion allocation for national AI upskilling in 2026 and DepEd’s AGAP.AI AI literacy rollout aimed at millions of learners, teachers and parents.  HPC / COARE and university HPC support (shared compute for AI research) DOST-ASTI’s COARE supercomputing and cloud services providing high performance computing resources and support to universities and research groups, and university data commons (UP Data Commons, DLSU projects) tied into national AI research capacity. - 1. Phillipines.Digital ID: DPI Map - PhilSys/ PhilID - The Philippine Identification System (PhilSys) is the Philippines’ national digital ID system that issues the PhilID and a unique identification number supported by biometric data for secure verification. Administered by the Philippine Statistics Authority, it aims to streamline public and private transactions while operating under the Data Privacy Act’s safeguards.
Phillipines.Digital Payments: DPI Map - PhilPASSPlus - The Philippine Payments and Settlements System Plus (PhilPaSSplus) is the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas’ upgraded real-time gross settlement system that enables high-value interbank fund transfers and settlement of financial market transactions in real time. Operated by the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas, it strengthens payment system resilience, liquidity management, and financial stability in the Philippines.
Phillipines.Data Exchange: DPI Map - eGovDX  InstaPay, PESONet & QR Ph - The eGovDX (eGovernment Data Exchange Platform) is the Philippines’ secure digital infrastructure that enables interoperable data sharing among government agencies to streamline public services. Led by the Department of Information and Communications Technology, it supports whole-of-government digital transformation while operating under data privacy and cybersecurity safeguards.  ̌InstaPay, PESONet, and QR Ph are electronic fund transfer and payment services in the Philippines mandated by the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) under the National Retail Payment System (NRPS). They provide secure, low-cost alternatives to cash, with InstaPay and QR Ph enabling real-time, low-value transactions, while PESONet is suited for high-value or batch transfers.

Bangladesh.AI Strategy: Government AI Readiness Index 2025 - Oxford Insights Digital Development Compass UNDP - National Artificial Intelligence Policy 2026-30  (Drafts V1.1 and V2.0) - Vision To develop an ethical and innovative environment for the use and development of AI to accelerate Bangladesh’s path towards a middle-income country, as outlined in the Vision 2041 master plan and aligned with the UN’s Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).   Mission  Establishing Bangladesh as a pioneer in AI innovation and adoption, creating a ‘Smart Bangladesh’ that leverages AI technologies for the well-being of all citizens, economic prosperity, and sustainable development in accord with four pillars; Smart Government, Smart Society, Smart Economy and Smart Citizen.  Objectives -Ethical and secure AI governance and compliance -Foster AI innovation for the public sector -Promoting economic growth -Strenghten AI readiness - National AI Policy Bangladesh 2026-2030 (DRAFT V1.1)  National AI Policy 2026-30 (Draft V2.0)
Bangladesh.AI Governance: UNESCO - Artificial Intelligence Readiness Assessment Report - As in AI Policy
Bangladesh.Data Legislation: DLA Piper UNDP Digital Development Compass - The Personal Data Protection Ordinance, 2025 - Vision Make provisions for processing personal data of a person for the purposes of legitimate use ensuring privacy, confidentiality and security and recognizing the personal data as personal right of a data-subject  Mission  Creating a unified framework that aligns with international standards and ensures privacy, confidentiality, and security of personal data, recognizing it as a right of the data subject.  Objectives  -Citizen focused control -Consent based processes -Digital sovereignty  -Grievance redressal -Responsible data fiduciary
Bangladesh.Government AI Initiatives: Government digital transformation portals, ministry annual reports, budget documents etc - MyGov Kagoj AI - MyGov  A one-stop service access point of Bangladesh for all govt. services by which citizens can avail of 172 digitsed services across 7 sectors by using their single identity.   Kagoj AI  Bangladesh's first Artificial Intelligence platform for the Bangla language; it supports AI driven language processing in Bangla and is useful in digitising existing written work using a new Julai font, that measures the compatability between English and Bangla.  Kagoj AI does not find itself limited to institutional and official use and can also be accessed and utilised by larger public audiences for content creation and digital writing  Enhancement of Bangla Language in ICT through Research & Development (EBLICT) Joint initiative by the ICT division and Bangaldesh Computer Council to increase inclusion of Bengali for AI development - MyGov  Government introduces Bangla-based AI platform and new font for official use
Bangladesh.Digital ID: DPI Map - Present: National ID (DPI like) - National ID (Ongoing)  The National ID is a smart ID card containing an embedded microchip and featuring a unique 10-digit identification number – the National Identification Number (NIN). It is used to access public and private services such as banking, mobile registration and voter registration among other purposes. From 2011-2018, the World Bank Identification for Enhanced Access to Services (IDEAS) Project provided technical assistance. Due to implementation and policy delays, only 90 million NID cards (half the desired goal) was achieved by 2018 - Bangladesh NID Application System
Bangladesh.Digital Payments: DPI Map - Present: National Payment Switch (DPI like) - National Payment Switch (Ongoing)  Operational since 2012, the National Payment Switch Bangladesh (NPSB) is a platform that allows for seamless movement of funds from customers to banks to Mobile Financial Services (MFS) providers. It   supports extensive bank-to-wallet and MFS-to-MFS transactions,  with an aim to replace existing banking platforms with a unified, secure system  Bank-to-MFS transfers fees set at Tk 1.5 per Tk 1,000; MFS-to-bank transfers set at Tk 8.5 per Tk 1,000; 2025 - NPSB: National Payment Switch Bangladesh   Bank-to-MFS and MFS-to-MFS money transfers via NPSB to start from 1 Nov | The Business Standard
Bangladesh.Data Exchange: DPI Map - Present: National E-Service Bus (DPI like) - National E-Service Bus (Ongoing)  As of a document from August 2025, the National E-Service Bus was being used by around 15 government agencies including the NID agency, National Board of Revenue, BTRC, the Ministry of Agriculture, and the Office of the Registrar General. The platform still requires upgrades for scaling and broader adoption. The World Bank group is assisting with the software upgrading of the bus, along with standardized API specifications, operational processes, and technical capacity for government agencies to leverage the platform for service delivery - BNDA National e-Service Bus   Project Information Document (PID)

Nepal.AI Strategy: Government AI Readiness Index 2025 - Oxford Insights Digital Development Compass UNDP - National AI Policy, 2025 - Vision To build a human-Centered, ethical, and prosperous Nepal through Artificial Intelligence (AI) technology.  Mission To maximize the use of Artificial Intelligence for the socio-economic development of the nation.  Goals - Increase the contribution of the information technology sector to the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) by 1% through the optimal use of AI across all socio-economic sectors. - Improve Nepal’s position within the top 50 countries in the Global Government AI Readiness Index. - Produce at least five thousand skilled human resources in the field of AI within five years. - Establish AI Excellence Centres in all provinces within five years. - Achieve AI literacy for the entire population, including students at the basic education level.  Objectives - Build a sustainable and reliable AI ecosystem. - Accelerate the economic growth rate through the optimal use of AI. - Enhance the effectiveness of public service delivery through AI applications. - Strengthen AI governance - National Artificial Intelligence (A.I.) Policy, 2025
Nepal.Data Legislation: Digital Development Compass UNDP - National Cyber Security Policy, 2023 - Building a secure as well as Resilient Cyber Space.  Mission To ensure information and data security and protection of information technology systems through a combination of people, process and technology by developing legal and institutional structure, enhancing public awareness and developing capacity.  Objectives -Establish legal and institutional frameworks to build a secure cyber space. -Protect critical national infrastructure while reducing the risk of cyberattacks. -Enhance cyber security research, human resource development and enhancing capacity of existing workforce, to ensure a robust and strong cyber space. -Improve the reliability and security of digital technology-based services. -Exchange bilateral, regional and international coordination, knowledge sharing, and assistance to reduce cyber security related risks.  Goal To increase Nepal's Global Cyber Security Index (GCI) score from the current 44.99% to 60% within the next 5 years, 70% within 10 years, and 80% in the 15 years.
Nepal.Government AI Initiatives: Government digital transformation portals, ministry annual reports, budget documents etc - Lalitpur Metropolitan City (LMC) has successfully deployed a pioneering AI-based intelligent traffic light system in certain major junctions in the city to manage the traffic situation more efficiently. This system uses real-time data to adjust timings at traffic signals automatically.  SITA - Developed by UNFPA, in collaboration with the Ministry of Health and Population, UNFPA HQ’s IT Services Office, Google, and with support from the British Embassy Kathmandu, SITA is an AI platform to  rapidly analyze national datasets and generate detailed insights. It scans thousands of variables in massive datasets like the Nepal Demographic and Health Survey (NDHS), runs the analysis, and outputs polished charts, tables, and even written narratives that match human quality. This helps in making real time decisions at national level. - https://nepaleconomicforum.org/a-view-on-the-national-ai-policy/  https://asiapacific.unfpa.org/en/news/sita-nepals-new-ai-tool-could-change-how-country-uses-data
Nepal.Digital ID: Digital Development Compass UNDP - Digital Nepal Framework, 2019 - Digital based Social economic prosperity  Mission To harness digital technologies as a catalyst to positively transform government, society and economy.  Objectives -To strengthen the foundation for a knowledge-based society and digital economy. -To provide a framework for action aimed at leveraging digital technologies to achieve Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).   Under the Digital Nepal framework, eight sectors – digital foundation, agriculture, health, education, energy, tourism, finance and urban infrastructure – and eighty initiatives have been identified to propel socioeconomic growth by unlocking their potential. - 2019 DIGITAL NEPAL FRAMEWORK
Nepal.Digital Payments: DPI Map - Rastriya Parichaya Patra; National Identity - Rastriya Parichaya Patra; National Identity  As of 2025, over 17 million people have been registered which is about 57% of the population and approximately 6 million cards have been printed. Over 97% of local government units have switched to online civil registration of vital events like births and deaths, forming a foundational database. The government has also started making the card mandatory for certain services, such as banking, social security fund access, and real estate transactions.   The ID system is integrated with the Nagarik App and is mandated (in 28 districts) for accessing services like pensions and health insurance. It aims to unify credentials like voter ID, citizenship cards, and driving licenses. However, there is no operational authentication infrastructure i.e. KYC and most agencies still require physical documents - Digitization of Identity in Nepal: Efforts, Experiences and Effects
Nepal.Data Exchange: DPI Map - National Payments Interface (NPI) - National Payments Interface (NPI) (Ongoing)  National Payments Interface (NPI) is the consolidated set of APIs of multiple payment systems hosted by NCHL or any other institutions built in an Open API platform concept. It currently provides access to the Nepal Clearing House Limited- Interbank Payment System (NCHL-IPS) and Retail Payment System (RPS); underlying payment systems with the overlaying services built on top of the platform.   Nepal Clearing House Limited (NCHL), a public limited company, serves as the core operator of the country’s clearing, settlement, and switching infrastructure and functions as the backbone of the National Payment Switch, enabling interoperability across systems.   It has also implemented initiatives such as NepalPay QR and NepalPay Instant for real-time payments. QR payments are accepted by over 2 million merchants, with volumes growing 117% in a year. In 2024/25, NCHL processed 186 million transactions, covering 17% of total market volume but 77% of value. Also ~90% of government expenses are digital transactions. - National Payments Interface (NPI) - Nepal Clearing House Limited
